title: 2026 U.S. Senate election in Michigan
description: Michigan’s 2026 U.S. Senate race is for an open seat after Sen. Gary Peters said he would not seek reelection. The contest matters because Michigan is a closely divided swing state and control of the Senate could hinge on the seat; the Democratic primary has featured a three-way affordability-focused race among Abdul El-Sayed, Mallory McMorrow, and Haley Stevens, while Republican Mike Rogers is the party’s leading contender.

candidates.name: Abdul El-Sayed
candidates.summary: Abdul El-Sayed is a physician and former Wayne County health director running for Michigan’s open U.S. Senate seat as a Democrat. He has emphasized affordability and health care in the race and has received an endorsement from Sen. Bernie Sanders.

Healthcare.stance: El-Sayed says he wants to expand Medicare to cover every American and explicitly backs Medicare for All. He frames health care as a right and argues that cash-assistance programs are helpful but not a substitute for universal insurance.

sources.title: A Healthier America - Abdul for U.S. Senate

Economy.stance: El-Sayed says he wants an economy that works for working people, with higher taxes on billionaires and corporations, stronger support for unions and small businesses, and policies aimed at lowering costs for everyday Michiganders.

sources.title: Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website
Abdul El-Sayed runs for Michigan's open U.S. Senate seat - WDET 101.9 FM

Climate/Energy.stance: El-Sayed has emphasized protecting Michigan’s water resources and limiting the impact of data centers on utility bills and energy use. His campaign messaging ties climate and energy policy to affordability, public health, and community benefits.

sources.title: Abdul El-Sayed runs for Michigan's open U.S. Senate seat - WDET 101.9 FM

Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website

Reproductive Rights.stance: El-Sayed has aligned himself with abortion rights and broader reproductive freedom in campaign messaging and public reporting. His campaign presents reproductive access as part of a broader civil-rights and public-health agenda.

sources.title: Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website
Differences within Democratic Party separate US Senate candidates

Immigration.stance: El-Sayed has called for abolishing ICE and sharply limiting its role, while saying immigration enforcement should exist in a different form. He has criticized the agency as overly militarized and abusive.

sources.title: 6 major issues in Michigan’s US Senate race

Guns & Safety.stance: El-Sayed says he supports common-sense gun safety measures and treats public safety as part of community health. His public campaign materials reviewed here do not provide a highly detailed gun policy beyond that broader framing.

sources.title: Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website

Foreign Policy.stance: El-Sayed’s campaign describes his approach as sensible foreign policy, but the materials reviewed do not lay out a detailed doctrine. Available messaging suggests a pragmatic and restrained posture rather than a highly specific foreign-policy agenda.

sources.title: Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website

Social Justice.stance: El-Sayed presents himself as a progressive candidate focused on civil rights, liberties, and building a government that works for everyday people rather than wealthy interests. His campaign materials emphasize equity, public services, and protecting rights.

sources.title: Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website

Education.stance: El-Sayed’s campaign says it supports more and better education, not less, and links education to affordability and opportunity for children. His public comments also connect education to preparing young people for future careers and a dignified life.

sources.title: Abdul for U.S. Senate | Official Campaign Website
Abdul El-Sayed runs for Michigan's open U.S. Senate seat - WDET 101.9 FM

Tech & AI.stance: El-Sayed has not, in the sources reviewed, released a detailed standalone tech or AI agenda. His campaign has discussed data centers, utility costs, and water use, which are the clearest technology-adjacent policy issues identified in the available material.

sources.title: Abdul El-Sayed runs for Michigan's open U.S. Senate seat - WDET 101.9 FM
6 major issues in Michigan’s US Senate race

Election Reform.stance: El-Sayed says the political system is too insulated from accountability and supports structural changes such as ending the filibuster and reforming the Supreme Court. He also frames his campaign as anti-corporate-money and pro-democracy.

Local Issues.stance: El-Sayed emphasizes Michigan-specific concerns such as housing costs, data centers, water use, electricity rates, and union jobs. He says data centers should not raise utility bills or drain water resources and should create good union jobs with enforceable standards.

Healthcare.stance: Stevens says she supports protecting and lowering health care costs, including extending Affordable Care Act subsidies and making coverage more accessible. She has also criticized RFK Jr. for endangering public health and cutting medical research funding.

Healthcare.stance: Rogers has been criticized by Michigan Democrats and news reports for opposing extension of ACA tax credits, which would raise costs for people with preexisting conditions. In the retrieved sources, his health care position is most clearly reflected by that opposition rather than a detailed campaign health care agenda.

sources.title: Michigan Democrats attack Rogers' opposition to extending ACA tax credits

Economy.stance: Rogers says he would bring manufacturing jobs back to Michigan, lower the cost of gas, groceries, and prescription drugs, cut government spending, and invest in skilled trades. He also says he will work with President Trump and protect Social Security for seniors.

sources.title: Mike Rogers for Senate
Mike Rogers announces bid for U.S. Senate

Climate/Energy.stance: Rogers has not released a detailed standalone climate agenda in the sources reviewed. His campaign messaging focuses more on manufacturing, energy affordability, and domestic production than on emissions or clean-energy targets.

sources.title: Mike Rogers for Senate
6 major issues in Michigan’s US Senate race

Reproductive Rights.stance: Rogers has a long record of supporting abortion restrictions, and outside groups and news reports describe him as having backed or lobbied for national abortion bans. The retrieved sources indicate he is opposed to abortion rights.

Mike Rogers wants voters to forget his record of backing abortion bans

Immigration.stance: Rogers has defended Trump’s immigration strategy and said keeping DHS fully funded is vital to national security. He has criticized efforts to cut or redirect immigration-enforcement funding and frames the issue as one of public safety and border/security enforcement.

sources.title: 6 major issues in Michigan’s US Senate race

Guns & Safety.stance: Rogers’s campaign materials reviewed here do not present a detailed gun-safety agenda. His site emphasizes law-and-order themes and public safety, but it does not specify positions such as background checks, assault-weapons bans, or red-flag laws on the campaign page reviewed.

sources.title: Mike Rogers for Senate

Foreign Policy.stance: Rogers presents himself as a national-security hawk, citing his Army, FBI, and House Intelligence Committee background and emphasizing fighting terrorists, protecting Americans, and standing with President Trump. His campaign messaging suggests a more assertive, pro-Trump foreign-policy posture, though the campaign site reviewed does not provide a detailed doctrine.

Social Justice.stance: Rogers’s campaign materials reviewed here do not lay out a social-justice platform. The site focuses on economic concerns, education, public safety, and backing Trump, rather than civil-rights, racial-equity, or justice-system reform proposals.

Education.stance: Rogers says he wants to prioritize education and set children up for success, but the campaign materials reviewed do not provide many specifics beyond that framing. His public launch messaging presents education as part of a broader message about preparing children for the future.

flags.concern: Five of Mallory McMorrow's top five donors are the same entity (Senate Democratic Fund of Michigan) listed in separate entries with different amounts ($90,000, $60,000, $52,800, $52,800, $52,800). This appears to be a data duplication or aggregation error rather than five distinct donations.
flags.suggestion: Consolidate these five entries into a single entry with clarified total or timeline, or verify with OpenSecrets that these are indeed separate contributions. If separate, add context (e.g., year or purpose).

flags.concern: Haley Stevens' reproductive-rights stance is marked as 'low' confidence with minimal sourcing. The summary states 'has not, in the sources reviewed, issued a detailed standalone campaign statement on abortion policy,' yet polling data shows voters care deeply about this issue in Michigan. This is a notable gap for a Democratic primary candidate.
flags.suggestion: Conduct additional research of Stevens' House voting record on reproductive rights (e.g., any votes on abortion-related bills), statements from her district office, or explicit campaign statements on the issue. If truly unavailable, note that the candidate has avoided detailed public positioning on this topic.

flags.concern: Rogers' climate/energy stance is marked 'low' confidence and lacks detail. Given Michigan's manufacturing base and recent data-center expansion (a topic other candidates discuss extensively), this represents an incomplete picture of his position on a locally salient issue.
flags.suggestion: Search for Rogers' statements on data centers, utility regulation, or energy policy in Michigan-specific news outlets or his congressional record. If truly absent, note explicitly that he has not articulated a detailed climate/energy platform in the 2026 race.

flags.concern: Rogers' healthcare stance cites only one source (a Democratic attack ad from Michigan Democrats). While factually accurate that he opposed ACA extension, sourcing his healthcare position solely through opposition messaging is somewhat one-sided.
flags.suggestion: Add Rogers' own campaign statement on healthcare, or note if he has not released a detailed healthcare agenda. Include any House votes on major healthcare bills (e.g., repeal efforts) to provide fuller context.

flags.concern: The Detroit News/WDIV poll (Jan 14, 2026) only includes one matchup (Rogers vs. Stevens, 44.1%–43.7%), while the Emerson poll (Jan 29) includes three Democratic primary matchups plus a three-way. The limited data from the earlier poll makes cross-candidate comparison difficult.
flags.suggestion: Note in context that this earlier poll's limited matchup scope reflects its smaller sample size (600 vs. 1000), or search for more complete Detroit News polling data if available.

flags.concern: Abdul El-Sayed's career timeline shows he was a 'Public health professor' at University of Michigan (2017–2019) while also serving as 'Executive Director' of Detroit Health Department (2015–2017). The dates do not overlap, but it's worth verifying he did not hold both roles simultaneously.
flags.suggestion: Confirm with official University of Michigan or Detroit Health Department records that these roles were sequential and non-overlapping.

reviews.summary: This is a comprehensive, well-sourced candidate profile for the 2026 Michigan Senate race. The profile demonstrates strong factual accuracy across all four candidates, with balanced and neutral language throughout. Career histories are accurate and properly documented via credible sources (Congress.gov, House.gov, Ballotpedia). The issues section is thorough, covering 12 policy areas per candidate with confidence ratings that appropriately reflect source availability. Sources are current (predominantly from 2025–2026), drawn from a strong mix of official campaign sites, credible news outlets (WDET, Bridge Michigan, Michigan Public), and government records. Polling data includes proper sample sizes and recent dates. Notable strengths: (1) Confidence ratings honestly reflect gaps (e.g., Rogers on Tech & AI marked 'low'); (2) issue stances are tied to specific candidate statements and voting records, not inferred; (3) the race description accurately reflects the open-seat contest and key Democratic primary dynamics; (4) language remains neutral even when describing controversial positions (e.g., Rogers' abortion record).

flags.concern: The year for Abdul El-Sayed's M.D. from the University of Michigan is listed as null, which may indicate incomplete information.
flags.suggestion: Verify and update with the actual graduation year from reliable sources to enhance completeness.

flags.concern: The year for Abdul El-Sayed's D.Phil. from the University of Oxford is listed as null, potentially leaving a gap in his educational background.
flags.suggestion: Confirm and add the correct year to provide a fuller picture of his qualifications.

flags.concern: Haley Stevens' stance on reproductive rights is described with low confidence and relies on only one source, which may not fully capture her position.
flags.suggestion: Incorporate additional sources, such as official statements or legislative records, to strengthen confidence and completeness.

flags.concern: Mike Rogers' stance on climate and energy is noted with low confidence and lacks detailed sourcing, potentially underrepresenting his views.
flags.suggestion: Add more specific sources, like recent interviews or policy documents, to improve accuracy and depth.

reviews.summary: This profile is a robust and meticulously compiled overview of the 2026 Michigan Senate race, with accurate representations of candidate positions drawn directly from credible sources like official campaign websites, Ballotpedia, and reputable news outlets such as WDET and Bridgemi. The inclusion of polling data, detailed career histories, and well-documented issue stances demonstrates thorough research, while the neutral language ensures a balanced, nonpartisan presentation across all candidates.
